title: Dimensional Analysis
subtitle: Dimensional analysis for Class 11 Physics — verify equations, derive formulae, convert units

blocks.heading: The Idea
blocks.body:
The principle of homogeneity says every term in a correct equation must have the same dimensions. You cannot add a length to a velocity.
This single idea lets you check equations, convert units, and even guess the form of a formula — all without doing the full physics.

blocks.heading: The Formula
blocks.body:
For any valid equation, the dimensions of the left side equal the dimensions of every term on the right:
Check v = u + at : \quad LT^{-1} = LT^{-1} + (LT^{-2})(T) = LT^{-1} ✓ — every term is a velocity, so it is dimensionally consistent.

blocks.heading: Real-World Example
blocks.body: In 1999 NASA lost the Mars Climate Orbiter because one team used pound-force and another used newtons — a units mismatch. A quick dimensional check is exactly the kind of sanity test engineers use to catch such blunders before they cost a spacecraft.

blocks.heading: Try It At Home
blocks.body: Take the pendulum formula T = 2\pi\sqrt{L/g}. Check it: \sqrt{L \div (LT^{-2})} = \sqrt{T^2} = T. The right side has the dimension of time, just like the period T. The formula is dimensionally sound.

blocks.heading: What If…
blocks.body: Dimensional analysis can give the form of a formula but never the pure number in front (like the 2\pi above). Why can dimensions never reveal a dimensionless constant?

quiz.question: In s = ut + ½at², checking the ½at² term gives which dimensions?
quiz.explanation: at² has dimensions (L T⁻²)(T²) = L, the same as displacement s. The ½ is just a dimensionless number, so the term is consistent.
